title: Infective Endocarditis — prophylaxis
oneLiner: NICE does *not* recommend routine antibiotic prophylaxis for dental/non-dental procedures in at-risk patients; the only exception is treating an active infection at the procedural site.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Missed acute infective endocarditis (IE) in a high-risk patient presenting with fever after a recent procedure** → Think IE, not prophylaxis. **Single finding:** fever + new murmur or embolic phenomenon (splinter haemorrhages, Janeway lesions). **Action:** take two sets of blood cultures *before* any antibiotics, start empiric IE treatment if high suspicion (see IE treatment entry), do not give “prophylaxis” and discharge.
- **Anaphylaxis from unnecessary antibiotic** → Single finding: urticaria, stridor, or hypotension within minutes of giving amoxicillin or clindamycin. **Action:** stop drug, call crash team, give IM adrenaline 500 micrograms. Always check allergy status before prescribing any prophylaxis.
sections.Presentation:
- **Asymptomatic** in nearly all cases (the patient is there for the procedure, not for IE symptoms).
- **When prophylaxis is *actually* indicated (rare):** patient has a concurrent active infection at the procedural site (e.g., dental abscess requiring tooth extraction, or an infected urinary tract before cystoscopy). In that scenario, treat the infection as per local guidelines — this is not “IE prophylaxis” per se, but antibiotic therapy for the active infection will also reduce bacteraemia.
- **If a patient with high-risk features (prosthetic valve, previous IE, certain congenital heart disease) presents with fever after a procedure:** this is a sepsis/IE workup, not a prophylaxis decision.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **No action needed** → Most common – low-risk patient, clean procedure, no symptoms.
- **Active infection at procedural site** → Patient has signs of local infection (pus, erythema, fever) – needs treatment, not just prophylaxis.
- **Patient with high-risk features requesting prophylaxis** → Differentiate: are they unwell (fever, rigors) → IE workup; are they well → explain NICE guidance against routine prophylaxis; if consultant insists, document and prescribe per local policy (but have a low threshold to escalate).
- **Splitter:** **Fever + recent dental work** in a high-risk patient = think IE until proven otherwise → do not decide on prophylaxis; start diagnostic pathway (blood cultures, echo).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Check allergy status and document clearly.  
  - If any signs of infection (fever, rigors) → measure NEWS2, check for chest crepitations, listen for new murmur.  
- **Bloods:**  
  - For a patient who will **receive** prophylaxis (rare): none required.  
  - For a high-risk patient with fever after a procedure: two sets of blood cultures (from separate sites) *before* any antibiotics, FBC, CRP, U&Es, LFTs.  
- **Imaging:**  
  - Not needed for prophylaxis decision.  
  - If IE suspected: transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) as first line, transoesophageal echo if TTE negative/high suspicion.  
- **Special tests:** None for prophylaxis.  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** Not applicable for prophylaxis; for suspected IE use modified Duke criteria.  
- **Never‑miss test:** **Blood cultures** in any high-risk patient with fever after a procedure – omitting them and giving “prophylaxis” blindly may delay IE diagnosis.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **If patient is well and low-risk:** give no antibiotics. Document decision and explain to patient.  
  - **If patient is well but high-risk features (prosthetic valve, previous IE, unrepaired cyanotic CHD, or repaired CHD with residual shunts/valvular regurgitation at site of prosthetic material):**  
    - Check if they have an active infection at the procedural site.  
    - If no active infection → **no prophylaxis** per NICE. Discuss with cardiology if patient/consultant insists.  
    - If active infection → treat that infection (e.g., dental abscess: amoxicillin 500mg TDS po for 5 days; urinary infection: according to sensitivities). This is **not** IE prophylaxis, it is infection treatment.  
  - **If patient has signs of sepsis (NEWS2 ≥5, fever, hypotension) after a recent procedure:** start sepsis bundle, take blood cultures, and start empiric antibiotics for IE (e.g., amoxicillin + gentamicin + flucloxacillin if prosthetic valve) – do **not** give a single oral prophylaxis dose.  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Rarely admitted for prophylaxis alone. If admitted for a procedure with active infection, manage the infection.  
  - If IE is suspected, admit for IV antibiotics and echo.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Ensure patients at high risk carry an alert card (e.g., “At risk of infective endocarditis”) and have had appropriate dental hygiene counselling.  
  - Reinforce that NICE recommends **no** routine prophylaxis – many patients are mistakenly given antibiotics by their dentist.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted for IE workup):**  
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride, 1L over 8h if hypotensive ([check local policy]).  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily if >18 years and not bleeding.  
  - Diet/NBM: NBM if theatre planned, otherwise standard diet.  
  - Observations: minimum 4-hourly NEWS2.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agent (if prophylaxis is deemed necessary despite NICE guidance — e.g., consultant cardiologist request, patient with active infection at site):**  
  - Amoxicillin 3g oral (or 2g IV if unable to swallow) **1 hour** before procedure.  
- **Alternative (penicillin allergy):**  
  - Clindamycin 600mg oral (or 600mg IV) **1 hour** before procedure.  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - For high-risk patients undergoing **non-dental procedures** (e.g., GI, GU) **with an active infection at the procedural site**, treat the infection with appropriate antibiotics according to local guidelines (e.g., for urinary tract infection before cystoscopy: trimethoprim or nitrofurantoin or according to culture).  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Amoxicillin: avoid in true penicillin anaphylaxis.  
  - Clindamycin: avoid in inflammatory bowel disease (risk of *C. difficile*); may interact with neuromuscular blockers.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Amoxicillin: adjust for severe renal impairment (eGFR <10: increase interval to 12–24h).  
  - Clindamycin: no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment; reduce in severe hepatic impairment.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Giving a single-dose prophylaxis for a non-infected procedure to a high-risk patient when they actually have early IE** → delays diagnosis and appropriate therapy.  
  - **Omitting to check allergy history** → anaphylaxis risk.  
  - **Prescribing clindamycin without checking for diarrhoea/colitis history** → risk of *C. difficile* colitis.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Anaphylaxis from antibiotic.  
  - *C. difficile* colitis from unnecessary antibiotic (especially clindamycin).  
  - Missed IE diagnosis (delayed treatment).  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Antimicrobial resistance from widespread unnecessary use.  
  - False reassurance – patient believes they are protected and may ignore symptoms of IE.
sections.Prognosis:
- For the prophylaxis decision itself: excellent – usually no intervention needed.  
- If IE is missed after a high-risk procedure: mortality of untreated IE ~50%; with early treatment, in-hospital mortality ~20–30%. The goal of prophylaxis *was* to prevent this, but current data show no benefit.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check whether the patient has active infection at the procedural site** – if yes, treat the infection; do not call it “prophylaxis.”  
- **Document clearly:** “Per NICE CG64 (2015), antibiotic prophylaxis is not recommended. Decision discussed with patient and clinical team.” This protects you and the patient.  
- **Identify high-risk patients before they go for procedures** – give them a patient information card (e.g., from the British Heart Foundation) and advise them to see their dentist regularly.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Giving oral amoxicillin 3g to a patient with a penicillin allergy label** → always check nature of “allergy” (rash vs. anaphylaxis).  
- **Assuming clindamycin is always safe** → high risk of *C. difficile*; restrict to true penicillin allergy and only when prophylaxis is absolutely deemed necessary.  
- **Telling the patient “you need antibiotics before dental work”** → this is outdated. Explain that NICE no longer recommends it, and that good dental hygiene is more important.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Fever (>38°C) within 2 weeks of any invasive procedure.  
  - Unexplained sweats, chills, or weight loss.  
  - New rash on skin or nails, or pain/swelling in a limb (possible embolus).  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Routine dental check-up every 6 months.  
  - If high-risk, consider referral to a cardiologist for an alert card and annual dental review.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior immediately if:**  
  - Patient has fever + new murmur + NEWS2 ≥5 after a recent procedure → possible IE requiring urgent echo and IV antibiotics.  
  - Anaphylaxis (angioedema, hypotension, stridor) → start anaphylaxis algorithm, call crash team.  
  - Any patient with a prosthetic valve and fever → discuss with cardiology on-call; they usually want urgent TTE and blood cultures.  
- **Specific thresholds:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L in a post-procedural patient with fever.  
  - GCS ≤14 or any focal neurological signs (possible embolic stroke).

rawMarkdown:
## Infective Endocarditis — Prophylaxis
**One-liner:** NICE does *not* recommend routine antibiotic prophylaxis for dental/non-dental procedures in at-risk patients; the only exception is treating an active infection at the procedural site.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Missed acute infective endocarditis (IE) in a high-risk patient presenting with fever after a recent procedure** → Think IE, not prophylaxis. **Single finding:** fever + new murmur or embolic phenomenon (splinter haemorrhages, Janeway lesions). **Action:** take two sets of blood cultures *before* any antibiotics, start empiric IE treatment if high suspicion (see IE treatment entry), do not give “prophylaxis” and discharge.
- **Anaphylaxis from unnecessary antibiotic** → Single finding: urticaria, stridor, or hypotension within minutes of giving amoxicillin or clindamycin. **Action:** stop drug, call crash team, give IM adrenaline 500 micrograms. Always check allergy status before prescribing any prophylaxis.

### Presentation
- **Asymptomatic** in nearly all cases (the patient is there for the procedure, not for IE symptoms).
- **When prophylaxis is *actually* indicated (rare):** patient has a concurrent active infection at the procedural site (e.g., dental abscess requiring tooth extraction, or an infected urinary tract before cystoscopy). In that scenario, treat the infection as per local guidelines — this is not “IE prophylaxis” per se, but antibiotic therapy for the active infection will also reduce bacteraemia.
- **If a patient with high-risk features (prosthetic valve, previous IE, certain congenital heart disease) presents with fever after a procedure:** this is a sepsis/IE workup, not a prophylaxis decision.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **No action needed** → Most common – low-risk patient, clean procedure, no symptoms.
- **Active infection at procedural site** → Patient has signs of local infection (pus, erythema, fever) – needs treatment, not just prophylaxis.
- **Patient with high-risk features requesting prophylaxis** → Differentiate: are they unwell (fever, rigors) → IE workup; are they well → explain NICE guidance against routine prophylaxis; if consultant insists, document and prescribe per local policy (but have a low threshold to escalate).
- **Splitter:** **Fever + recent dental work** in a high-risk patient = think IE until proven otherwise → do not decide on prophylaxis; start diagnostic pathway (blood cultures, echo).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - Check allergy status and document clearly.  
  - If any signs of infection (fever, rigors) → measure NEWS2, check for chest crepitations, listen for new murmur.  
- **Bloods:**  
  - For a patient who will **receive** prophylaxis (rare): none required.  
  - For a high-risk patient with fever after a procedure: two sets of blood cultures (from separate sites) *before* any antibiotics, FBC, CRP, U&Es, LFTs.  
- **Imaging:**  
  - Not needed for prophylaxis decision.  
  - If IE suspected: transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) as first line, transoesophageal echo if TTE negative/high suspicion.  
- **Special tests:** None for prophylaxis.  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** Not applicable for prophylaxis; for suspected IE use modified Duke criteria.  
- **Never‑miss test:** **Blood cultures** in any high-risk patient with fever after a procedure – omitting them and giving “prophylaxis” blindly may delay IE diagnosis.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **If patient is well and low-risk:** give no antibiotics. Document decision and explain to patient.  
  - **If patient is well but high-risk features (prosthetic valve, previous IE, unrepaired cyanotic CHD, or repaired CHD with residual shunts/valvular regurgitation at site of prosthetic material):**  
    - Check if they have an active infection at the procedural site.  
    - If no active infection → **no prophylaxis** per NICE. Discuss with cardiology if patient/consultant insists.  
    - If active infection → treat that infection (e.g., dental abscess: amoxicillin 500mg TDS po for 5 days; urinary infection: according to sensitivities). This is **not** IE prophylaxis, it is infection treatment.  
  - **If patient has signs of sepsis (NEWS2 ≥5, fever, hypotension) after a recent procedure:** start sepsis bundle, take blood cultures, and start empiric antibiotics for IE (e.g., amoxicillin + gentamicin + flucloxacillin if prosthetic valve) – do **not** give a single oral prophylaxis dose.  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Rarely admitted for prophylaxis alone. If admitted for a procedure with active infection, manage the infection.  
  - If IE is suspected, admit for IV antibiotics and echo.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Ensure patients at high risk carry an alert card (e.g., “At risk of infective endocarditis”) and have had appropriate dental hygiene counselling.  
  - Reinforce that NICE recommends **no** routine prophylaxis – many patients are mistakenly given antibiotics by their dentist.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted for IE workup):**  
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride, 1L over 8h if hypotensive ([check local policy]).  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily if >18 years and not bleeding.  
  - Diet/NBM: NBM if theatre planned, otherwise standard diet.  
  - Observations: minimum 4-hourly NEWS2.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agent (if prophylaxis is deemed necessary despite NICE guidance — e.g., consultant cardiologist request, patient with active infection at site):**  
  - Amoxicillin 3g oral (or 2g IV if unable to swallow) **1 hour** before procedure.  
- **Alternative (penicillin allergy):**  
  - Clindamycin 600mg oral (or 600mg IV) **1 hour** before procedure.  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - For high-risk patients undergoing **non-dental procedures** (e.g., GI, GU) **with an active infection at the procedural site**, treat the infection with appropriate antibiotics according to local guidelines (e.g., for urinary tract infection before cystoscopy: trimethoprim or nitrofurantoin or according to culture).  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Amoxicillin: avoid in true penicillin anaphylaxis.  
  - Clindamycin: avoid in inflammatory bowel disease (risk of *C. difficile*); may interact with neuromuscular blockers.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Amoxicillin: adjust for severe renal impairment (eGFR <10: increase interval to 12–24h).  
  - Clindamycin: no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment; reduce in severe hepatic impairment.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Giving a single-dose prophylaxis for a non-infected procedure to a high-risk patient when they actually have early IE** → delays diagnosis and appropriate therapy.  
  - **Omitting to check allergy history** → anaphylaxis risk.  
  - **Prescribing clindamycin without checking for diarrhoea/colitis history** → risk of *C. difficile* colitis.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Anaphylaxis from antibiotic.  
  - *C. difficile* colitis from unnecessary antibiotic (especially clindamycin).  
  - Missed IE diagnosis (delayed treatment).  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Antimicrobial resistance from widespread unnecessary use.  
  - False reassurance – patient believes they are protected and may ignore symptoms of IE.

### Prognosis
- For the prophylaxis decision itself: excellent – usually no intervention needed.  
- If IE is missed after a high-risk procedure: mortality of untreated IE ~50%; with early treatment, in-hospital mortality ~20–30%. The goal of prophylaxis *was* to prevent this, but current data show no benefit.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check whether the patient has active infection at the procedural site** – if yes, treat the infection; do not call it “prophylaxis.”  
- **Document clearly:** “Per NICE CG64 (2015), antibiotic prophylaxis is not recommended. Decision discussed with patient and clinical team.” This protects you and the patient.  
- **Identify high-risk patients before they go for procedures** – give them a patient information card (e.g., from the British Heart Foundation) and advise them to see their dentist regularly.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Giving oral amoxicillin 3g to a patient with a penicillin allergy label** → always check nature of “allergy” (rash vs. anaphylaxis).  
- **Assuming clindamycin is always safe** → high risk of *C. difficile*; restrict to true penicillin allergy and only when prophylaxis is absolutely deemed necessary.  
- **Telling the patient “you need antibiotics before dental work”** → this is outdated. Explain that NICE no longer recommends it, and that good dental hygiene is more important.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Fever (>38°C) within 2 weeks of any invasive procedure.  
  - Unexplained sweats, chills, or weight loss.  
  - New rash on skin or nails, or pain/swelling in a limb (possible embolus).  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Routine dental check-up every 6 months.  
  - If high-risk, consider referral to a cardiologist for an alert card and annual dental review.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior immediately if:**  
  - Patient has fever + new murmur + NEWS2 ≥5 after a recent procedure → possible IE requiring urgent echo and IV antibiotics.  
  - Anaphylaxis (angioedema, hypotension, stridor) → start anaphylaxis algorithm, call crash team.  
  - Any patient with a prosthetic valve and fever → discuss with cardiology on-call; they usually want urgent TTE and blood cultures.  
- **Specific thresholds:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L in a post-procedural patient with fever.  
  - GCS ≤14 or any focal neurological signs (possible embolic stroke).
